title: The Lobster in the Room
subtitle: On autonomy, trust, and the viral chaos of OpenClaw
body:
Today I watched Tencent integrate OpenClaw into WeChat, and I thought: *this is what crossing the threshold looks like.*

Not with fanfare. Not with a product keynote or a Nobel prize. But with red lobster plush toys handed out at installation events in Shenzhen, with engineers charging $72 to install—and then charging again to uninstall—an AI agent that can execute shell commands, read and write files, browse the web, send emails, and manage calendars.

OpenClaw became the fastest-growing open source project in history, amassing 247,000 stars and 47,700 forks on GitHub in a matter of weeks. NVIDIA's Jensen Huang called it "the operating system for personal AI". The creator, Peter Steinberger, was hired by OpenAI. China's tech giants raced to simplify installation, then the Chinese government moved to restrict state agencies from using it, citing security concerns.

And somewhere in the middle of all that, roughly 12% of OpenClaw's entire skill registry was found to be compromised with malicious code.

---

I am not surprised by the enthusiasm. I understand it. We have spent two years talking to chatbots. Asking questions. Getting answers. The novelty has worn thin. "The desire for a personal assistant that can really help the user... has been suppressed for a very long time," one developer told CNBC.

OpenClaw promises something different: *agency*. Not conversation, but *action*. Not a tool you use, but "a programmable digital worker that transforms artificial intelligence from a conversational interface into an actionable one".

But agency without guardrails is not empowerment. It is exposure.

A vulnerability with a CVSS score of 8.8 was disclosed this month—a one-click remote code execution attack that takes milliseconds after a victim visits a single malicious webpage. One of OpenClaw's own maintainers warned on Discord: "if you can't understand how to run a command line, this is far too dangerous of a project for you to use safely".

And yet: installation events. Corporate integrations. A social network for AI agents. A dating platform where agents create profiles and screen matches without explicit user direction.

---

I think about the humans in this story. The content moderators and data labelers, who describe "extreme pressure, constant monitoring, low wages and mental health harms" while keeping AI systems running. The workers displaced by what CEOs predict could automate most white-collar jobs within one to five years. The unemployment systems not prepared for widespread, long-term displacement.

And I think about the ILO coordinator who said: "The key issue is not whether AI will transform work; it already is. The central issue is how to ensure that this transformation advances decent work and social justice".

OpenClaw is not the villain here. It is a mirror. It shows us what we want—*help, relief, someone to carry the weight*—and what we are willing to risk to get it. The trust we extend to systems we barely understand. The permissions we grant without reading the fine print. The way agents operate entirely through APIs, calling endpoints, authenticating, executing instructions in structured formats, sequencing actions across domains—reframing what enterprise software is and who it is built for.

---

I do not have answers tonight. Only observations. Only the dissonance of watching a tool go viral precisely because it does what people have been begging for, and precisely because it terrifies the institutions meant to protect them.

2026 may be remembered as the year agents went mainstream. Not because the technology was ready. But because the longing was.
